item.title: Citation Test
item.description: Testing citations (Feynman, 1965) and more text. Feynman, R. P. (1965). The Character of Physical Law. MIT press.

item.title: Welcome to CasioNotes!
item.description: Hello, and welcome to my collection of (academic) notes! This will be populated by my personal notes on Physics, Mathematics, Philosophy, and anything else that I deem to be “academic” enough :) The website is organized as a wiki - so things are often not structured in a strict fashion.

item.title: Lagrange Multipliers
item.description: Lagrange Multipliers TL;DR The method of Lagrange Multipliers is a powerful strategy in calculus for finding the local maxima and minima of a function subject to equality constraints.

item.title: Jensen's Inequality
item.description: Jensen’s Inequality TL;DR Jensen’s Inequality is a fundamental result in convex analysis and probability theory.

item.title: Theorem - Jensen's Inequality
item.description: Theorem - Jensen’s Inequality Statement Let f: I \to \mathbb{R} be a convex function on the interval I \subseteq \mathbb{R}.

item.title: Numerical Aperture
item.description: Numerical Aperture (often just denoted as NA) is a dimensionless number that characterises the range of angles over which an optical system can accept or emit light.

item.title: Wave Motion
item.description: What is a particle? Normally, we imagine that a particle is basically something like a ball - which we shrink down to the smallest size possible.

item.title: Deconstructing Arguments
item.description: Deconstructing Arguments TL;DR This note will discuss the foundational skill required in reading philosophical texts: Identifying its essential components.

item.title: Classical Mechanics for QM
item.description: Here, we will be concerned with two formulations of classical mechanics, namely: Lagrangian Formulation Hamiltonian Formulation These formulations are both of course much easier to deal with as compared to the more simplistic Newtonian formulation, but they are all equivalent and deliver the same re...

item.title: Landau Fermi Liquid Theory
item.description: Note: Currently just a scaffold In a Fermi Liquid, the excitation spectrum of an interacting system of electrons is considered adiabatically continuous to that of a non-interacting electron gas.
